food_name: Tempe Goreng Sambal (Fried Tempeh with Sambal)
region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia — widely adopted across Singapore Malay hawker stalls
food_subgroup: Deep-fried fermented soybean cake with chilli-based sambal coating

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a concept for tempeh (fermented soybean cake) but does not have a specific concept for deep-fried tempeh with sambal chilli coating. FOODON_03309998 (tempeh) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried fermented soybean tempeh with sambal chilli sauce coating
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tempe goreng sambal. A00YE (tempeh/fermented soybean) used as closest parent with preparation descriptor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for tempe goreng sambal or fried tempeh with sambal as of 2026.

serving.description: Side dish serving — approximately 3–4 pieces of fried tempeh with sambal coating
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of Tempe Goreng Sambal is approximately 80g (3–4 pieces of fried tempeh with sambal coating), as commonly served at Indonesian hawker stalls and Singaporean Malay nasi padang counters.

nutrient_source.title: Tempe Goreng Sambal (fried tempeh with sambal) — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for tempeh base values and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for sambal component
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore (Foodo); USDA Agricultural Research Service (USDA SR Legacy); Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for fried tempeh with sambal. Cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 16114, tempeh, cooked) for tempeh base macros and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for sambal chilli paste component. Values represent the composite prepared dish including frying oil absorption and sambal coating.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tempeh and Fermented Soybean Products — University of Sydney GI Database; tempeh GI ~25 (low) based on fermented soy matrix
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tempeh has a well-established GI of approximately 25 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database, attributable to the dense fermented soy protein-fibre matrix that slows carbohydrate digestion. Tempe Goreng Sambal uses tempeh as the primary carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The sambal coating adds minor palm sugar (contributing to the 4.5g sugar/100g) but does not materially alter the overall GI. GL per 80g serving = (25 × 12.5 × 0.80) / 100 = 2.5 (very low). GI assigned as analogous estimate from tempeh reference food.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium from shrimp paste (belacan) and salt in sambal coating
hypertension.guidance: An 80g serving delivers approximately 304mg sodium — individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or extra sambal when consuming this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~25) and very low glycemic load per serving (GL 2.5) due to fermented soy protein-fibre matrix
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tempe Goreng Sambal is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes at a standard 80g serving; the fermented soybean matrix produces a very low glycaemic response, and the high protein (15.8g/100g) and fibre (4.8g/100g) content further blunts postprandial glucose rise, though the palm sugar in sambal should be noted for strict carbohydrate counters.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fat profile from soy with low saturated fat (3.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, and cardioprotective soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tempe Goreng Sambal provides a heart-friendly plant protein source with zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (3.2g/100g), and soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) that have demonstrated LDL-lowering properties; sodium at 380mg/100g warrants portion awareness for those managing blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones, high magnesium (62mg/100g), and selenium (12mcg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tempeh provides isoflavones with anti-inflammatory properties and the fermentation process enhances bioavailability of zinc and iron; this dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of a balanced anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tempe Goreng Sambal is one of the most beloved and widely consumed side dishes in both Indonesian (particularly Javanese) and Singaporean Malay cuisine. Slabs or bite-sized pieces of tempeh — Indonesia's traditional fermented soybean cake made by culturing cooked soybeans with Rhizopus mould — are deep-fried in hot oil until golden, crispy, and caramelised on the outside, then tossed or coated in a fragrant, spicy-sweet sambal. The sambal is made from ground dried chilli, onion, galangal, shrimp paste (belacan/terasi), and palm sugar, producing an intensely flavourful, slightly sticky, caramelised coating that clings to the crispy tempeh. The texture contrast between the crunchy fried exterior and the dense, nutty tempeh interior is a defining characteristic. This record covers the fully prepared and cooked form — not raw or uncooked tempeh. Nutritionally, Tempe Goreng Sambal is a powerhouse hawker side dish: it delivers 15.8g protein per 100g from the fermented soy base, 4.8g dietary fibre, 92mg calcium, 62mg magnesium, and meaningful B-vitamin content including riboflavin (0.32mg), niacin (3.5mg), and folate (42mcg). The fermentation process enhances bioavailability of zinc, iron, and B12 (0.08mcg). Deep frying adds fat (16.5g/100g total, 3.2g saturated) and the sambal contributes sodium (380mg/100g) and minor sugar (4.5g/100g from palm sugar). The glycaemic index is very low (~25) due to the dense fermented soy protein-fibre matrix, making this dish suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Cholesterol is zero. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A standard side dish serving is 80g (approximately 3–4 pieces), delivering approximately 206 kcal and a glycaemic load of only 2.5.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary regional source) for fried tempeh with sambal, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for tempeh base macros and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for sambal component. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for tempeh as analogous estimate. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from approved sources. Confidence set to high as the dish is well-documented in the HPB Foodo database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for tempe goreng sambal (composite fried dish with sambal) is not separately reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level as of 2026; individual fatty acid fractions for the composite prepared dish are not available. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite prepared dish is not reported in the primary source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy — tempeh is a plant-based food with no cholesterol; confirmed zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — tempeh and sambal ingredients contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate based on tempeh reference food from the University of Sydney GI Database; the sambal coating (palm sugar contribution) does not materially alter the overall GI of the dish. Sodium of 380mg/100g reflects the sambal coating containing shrimp paste (belacan) and salt; individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size. The dish is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians due to shrimp paste (belacan) in the sambal. Hindi local name (तेम्पे गोरेंग सम्बल) is a transliteration as this dish is not traditionally named in Hindi; included to satisfy Singaporean multilingual naming requirements.
